claim_text: In the currently coded Ohio matched triads (Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati, Toledo), police and fire agreements more consistently show interest-arbitration / formal-impasse-backstop language than their matched non-safety agreements.
claim_scope: Ohio; police and fire vs. matched 'other' unit; 2022-2025 cycles
evidence_strength: Pattern observed across all four coded Ohio matched triads in the evidence viewer, not a single-city artifact; consistent with Ohio's statutory public-safety impasse framework.
key_limitations: Not yet cross-checked row-by-row against the matched non-safety unit's own arbitration/impasse coding in every triad; counterevidence_ids not yet populated (needs a deliberate pass to find or rule out non-safety impasse language before this claim can move past draft).
additional_sources_needed: A systematic per-triad comparison table (safety vs. matched non-safety
report_ready: same attribute) pulled directly from gabriel_codify_evidence_layer.csv
column17: Illustrative draft row populated from real Ohio obs_ids and evidence_ids already in the corpus; not yet verified against every non-safety counterpart.

claim_text: Texas evidence suggests safety wage-setting channels are more institutionally specialized than non-safety channels, but the non-safety comparison remains weaker outside Houston.
key_limitations: Houston has a full matched non-safety comparison ('other' unit) with its own coded arbitration/impasse language, but San Antonio and Austin's non-safety coverage is thinner (Austin's non-safety comparison is nurse_health, not a direct clerical/public-works match; San Antonio has no coded non-safety unit at all in the current corpus).
report_ready: San Antonio has no matched non-safety contract yet in data/contracts.csv (flagged as an unmatched safety city in city_coverage.csv), so the 'weaker outside Houston' half of this claim is really a data gap, not yet a confirmed institutional finding.
notes: A matched non-safety unit for San Antonio (clerical_admin or public_works)
column17: and a closer occupational match for Austin's non-safety comparison
column19: Illustrative draft row; flags San Antonio's missing non-safety match as the binding constraint on this claim rather than asserting the comparison is confirmed weak.

claim_text: Across the current corpus, the strongest evidence pattern is not generic job difficulty, but the combination of occupational pressure (staffing shortage / mandatory overtime) with bargaining/impasse/premium-pay conversion mechanisms, concentrated in safety units.
claim_scope: Cross-state (MA/TX/OH); police and fire vs. matched non-safety; full current corpus
reasoning_summary: Motivated by the final mechanism-evidence report's headline framing (docs/final_reports/deeper_look_safety_non_safety_wage_mechanisms_2026-07-10.*), which observed this co-occurrence pattern narratively but did not formalize it as a scored claim with an explicit evidence count.
additional_sources_needed: This claim was written narratively in the prior mechanism-inventory report but has not yet been re-derived as a structured claim-to-evidence count (how many safety units show the co-occurrence vs. how many matched non-safety units do); risk of restating the prior report's framing without independently re-verifying it against the register.
report_ready: A structured co-occurrence count (per unit: does it have both an occupational-pressure attribute AND a bargaining/impasse/premium-pay attribute coded present) run directly off gabriel_codify_evidence_layer.csv
column17: Illustrative draft row; this is the highest-priority claim to formalize first since it is the closest to becoming the report's headline finding.

claim_text: Massachusetts's Seekonk and Franklin matched triads show broader occupational coverage (clerical, teacher, public works, library, plus police/fire) than any Texas or Ohio matched city, which may make Massachusetts the strongest source of non-safety comparison depth in the current corpus.
claim_scope: Massachusetts; Seekonk and Franklin multi-occupation triads
reasoning_summary: This is a corpus-composition observation directly countable from data/contracts.csv (occupation_class per city), not a mechanism-evidence claim — it does not require codify output to verify, only a city-by-occupation count.
additional_sources_needed: This is a structural/coverage claim, not yet a mechanism claim; 16 Massachusetts contracts remain uncodified per PROGRESS.md, so mechanism-level comparison depth (not just occupational coverage) for these triads is not yet established.
report_ready: Codify the remaining ~16 uncodified Massachusetts contracts to convert this coverage observation into a mechanism-comparison claim
column17: Marked supported_provisional because the underlying occupational-coverage count is directly verifiable from data/contracts.csv today, unlike C01-C03 which need further evidence-layer work; still provisional because it is a coverage claim, not yet a mechanism-strength claim.
